> **Goal:** learn to *measure* a model honestly (beyond accuracy) and to *cure*
> overfitting with regularization — the two skills that separate a model that
> looks good from a model that actually works on new data.

## Why a mathematician should care

A trained model is a hypothesis; evaluation is the experiment that tests it. A
single number like accuracy can flatter a useless model, so we need metrics that
separate the *kinds* of mistakes. And when a model overfits, regularization adds
one term to the cost — a penalty on the weights — that mathematically trades a
little bias for a lot less variance. Both ideas are pure linear algebra and
calculus applied with care.

## What is in the notebooks

Two notebooks, pure NumPy + Matplotlib (with optional brief scikit-learn
comparisons). Run each code cell with **Shift + Enter**.

- `14a_evaluation_metrics.ipynb` — confusion matrix, precision/recall/F1, the
  threshold trade-off and ROC, regression metrics, and cross-validation.
- `14b_regularization.ipynb` — ridge (L2), lasso (L1), the $\lambda$ U-curve,
  bias–variance in terms of $\lambda$, and weight decay.

## Core ideas in this chapter

### Why accuracy misleads
- On **imbalanced** data a do-nothing model can score 99% and catch zero
  positives. Accuracy hides *which* errors happen.

### The confusion matrix
- Every binary prediction is **TP / FP / FN / TN**.
- All other classification metrics are arithmetic on these four counts.

### Precision, recall, F1
- **Precision** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ — punishes false alarms.
- **Recall** $= \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ — punishes misses.
- **F1** $= 2\dfrac{P\cdot R}{P+R}$ — harmonic mean; high only when both are.
- Recall matters when a miss is costly (disease, fraud); precision when a false
  alarm is costly (spam filtering).

### The threshold trade-off
- A classifier outputs a score $\hat p$; predict 1 when $\hat p \ge t$.
- Lowering $t$ raises recall and lowers precision (and vice versa).
- The **ROC curve** plots TPR vs FPR across all thresholds; **AUC** summarizes it
  (1.0 perfect, 0.5 random).

### Regression metrics
- **MSE** $=\frac1n\sum(\hat y_i-y_i)^2$; **RMSE** $=\sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}$ (same
  units); **MAE** $=\frac1n\sum|\hat y_i-y_i|$ (robust);
  **R²** $=1-\dfrac{\sum(\hat y_i-y_i)^2}{\sum(y_i-\bar y)^2}$ (variance
  explained; mean-predictor scores 0).

### Validation done right
- Split **train / validation / test**: fit on train, tune hyperparameters on
  validation, touch test **once**.
- **k-fold cross-validation** (from scratch): split into $k$ folds, train on
  $k-1$ and validate on the held-out fold, then **average**. Stable, uses all
  data, and is how we pick hyperparameters.

### Overfitting and L2 (ridge)
- Overfit models have **huge weights**. Ridge penalizes them:
  $$J(\theta) = \tfrac1n\|X\theta - y\|^2 + \lambda\|\theta\|^2.$$
- Closed form — the **modified normal equation**:
  $$\theta = (X^\top X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^\top y.$$
- Larger $\lambda$ → smaller weights → smoother fit; sweeping $\lambda$ traces a
  validation **U-curve** whose bottom is the best strength.

### L1 (lasso) and sparsity
- Penalty $\lambda\|\theta\|_1$ drives weights to **exactly zero**, performing
  automatic **feature selection**. Its (sub)gradient $\lambda\,\mathrm{sign}(\theta)$
  does not vanish at 0, so it pushes weights all the way down; L2 only shrinks.

### Bias–variance in terms of $\lambda$
- $\text{test error} = \text{bias}^2 + \text{variance} + \text{noise}$.
- Small $\lambda$: low bias, high variance (overfit). Large $\lambda$: high bias,
  low variance (underfit). $\lambda$ is a continuous dial for the trade-off.

### Weight decay
- Adding the L2 gradient $2\lambda\theta$ turns the update into
  $\theta \leftarrow (1 - 2\eta\lambda)\theta - \eta\nabla\mathrm{MSE}$ — each
  step shrinks the weights before descending. This is L2 = **weight decay**
  (connects to Chapter 12).

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| 99% accuracy | can mean the model caught **zero** of the rare class |
| Precision vs recall | you usually cannot maximize both — pick by cost of errors |
| Threshold 0.5 | just a default; moving it slides precision against recall |
| Test set leakage | tuning on the test set quietly makes its score dishonest |
| R² = 0 | not "bad luck" — it's exactly the mean-predictor baseline |
| Bigger $\lambda$ | helps until it over-shrinks and *underfits* |
| L1 vs L2 | L1 zeroes weights out; L2 only shrinks them |

## Building the notebooks

These notebooks are generated from a Python script (never hand-edited JSON):

```
python tools/generators/ch14_eval_regularization.py
```

## After this chapter

You can now judge a model honestly and tame overfitting. These habits —
metrics-by-error-type, cross-validation, and a regularization penalty — carry
over to every model in the rest of the course, from logistic regression to deep
neural networks.

## 📚 Further reading

- Andrew Ng — [Machine Learning Specialization](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/machine-learning-introduction) (Coursera, DeepLearning.AI) — bias–variance and regularization explained.
- [scikit-learn documentation](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/) — metrics, cross-validation, and ridge/lasso APIs.
- [Deep Learning book](https://www.deeplearningbook.org) — Goodfellow, Bengio & Courville (free) — chapter 7 on regularization.
